title: Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Book notes


Detailed study notes on Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory: Effective Use of Theories of Change and Logic Models (Funnell and Rogers 2011). The notes are organized by the book’s five-part structure and designed for later expansion into a multi-book evaluation knowledge base.

16 chapter notes 5 parts Theory of change Logic models





Synthesis


Review recurring distinctions such as theory of change, theory of action, logic models, assumptions, and causal inference.



Map


Use the part and chapter sequence as a reading map for the book.



Practice


Translate the book into practical habits for evaluation design, monitoring, causal analysis, and reporting.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Program Theory
Program theory is the explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to change. Across the book, it functions as a bridge between design, implementation, monitoring, evaluation, causal inference, and reporting. The recurring discipline is to make assumptions visible enough that they can be discussed, tested, revised, and used.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
section: Theory of Change and Theory of Action
text:
Theory of Change and Theory of Action
The book separates the theory of change from the theory of action. The theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem conditions to intended outcomes. The theory of action explains what the program does to activate that pathway: resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, implementation conditions, and assumptions about factors inside and outside the program’s control.
This distinction matters because a program may have a plausible desired change pathway but weak activities, or strong activities that do not address the causal factors identified in the situation analysis. The two theories need to be checked against each other.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Logic Models and Representation
Logic models are representations of program theory, not the theory itself. Pipeline models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and systems-informed diagrams each foreground different features. The right representation depends on intended use, intervention complexity, and audience.
A good representation explains contribution, makes links meaningful, avoids dead ends, shows direction of change, and removes detail that does not help interpretation.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Assumptions and Context
Assumptions appear throughout the book as testable claims about how change happens, what context permits, and what factors affect success. Program theory is strongest when assumptions are explicit enough to guide evidence collection and humble enough to be revised.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Causal Inference
Program theory supports causal inference by organizing congruence analysis, counterfactual comparison, and critical review. It does not replace causal evidence; it makes causal claims more inspectable by showing what patterns should be observed if the theory is operating.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 528-545 Chapter 16

This note summarises Chapter 16, “Synthesis and Reporting,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 528-545 Chapter 16

This note summarises Chapter 16, “Synthesis and Reporting,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Core Argument
Synthesis and reporting should use program theory to integrate diverse evidence, explain causal contribution, support timely learning, and connect findings across linked evaluations.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Study Summary
The final chapter focuses on how evidence is brought together and reported. For a single evaluation, program theory can organize diverse evidence along the outcomes chain, helping readers see how implementation, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, context, and assumptions relate.
The authors also discuss rapid reporting and software-supported reporting. The key issue is not format but usefulness: reporting should make the evidence traceable to the theory and support decisions while the information is still actionable.
Across evaluations, program theory provides a structure for synthesis. It can connect findings from linked projects, compare evidence across different evaluations, and build cumulative understanding about what works, for whom, why, and under what conditions.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Key Concepts

Theory-based synthesis: integrating evidence according to the causal structure of the program theory.
Rapid reporting: timely communication designed to support ongoing decisions.
Cross-evaluation synthesis: using program theory to compare and integrate findings across studies.
Traceability: making clear how evidence supports conclusions and judgements.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Synthesis And Reporting For A Single Evaluation
Synthesizing Diverse Evidence Along the Outcomes Chain
Rapid Reporting
Software to Report Evidence in Terms of a Logic Model
Synthesis And Reporting Across Evaluations
Synthesis Across Linked Projects
Synthesis Across Different Evaluations
Summary
Exercises
N E W F R O N T I E R S F O R P R O G R A M T H E O R Y

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Practical Implications

Structure reports around the theory when the theory is central to the evaluation.
Show where evidence is strong, weak, missing, or contradictory along the causal chain.
Use synthesis to refine theories for future programs and evaluations.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 444-495 Chapter 14

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 444-495 Chapter 14

This note summarises Chapter 14, “Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Core Argument
Program theory can anchor monitoring and evaluation plans by identifying what to measure, when to measure it, what comparisons are needed, and how evaluation questions connect to the expected causal pathway.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shows how program theory becomes operational in performance monitoring and evaluation design. It helps decide which aspects of the situation, outcomes, success criteria, assumptions, implementation features, and causal links should be measured.
The authors stress choice. A theory may contain many possible indicators, but a monitoring system must prioritize measures that are feasible, useful, and tied to decisions. Evaluation planning then uses the theory to identify timing, purposes, key questions, information needs, methods, and comparisons.
The chapter also considers complicated and complex programs. In such cases, evaluation may need to examine variation across strands, sites, contexts, or emergent pathways rather than assume one uniform model. Program theory is useful when it guides these design choices rather than becoming a static appendix.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Key Concepts

Performance monitoring: routine tracking of selected aspects of implementation, context, outputs, outcomes, and assumptions.
Evaluation questions: focused questions that determine evidence needs and methods.
Comparison: a design feature used to interpret performance or causal contribution.
Theory-based measurement: choosing measures because they test or illuminate the program theory.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Using Program Theory For Performance Monitoring
Making Choices About What To Measure Within The Program Theory
Which Aspects of the Situation to Measure
Which Outcomes in the Chain of Outcomes to Measure
Choices Concerning Which Success Criteria to Monitor and Which to Evaluate
Choices Concerning Factors That Affect Success
Choices Concerning Aspects of Program Implementation to Measure
Choices Concerning Links in the Program Theory to Measure
Including Comparisons As Part Of The Performance Information System
Using Program Theory To Plan An Evaluation
Step 1: Timing and Purposes of an Evaluation
Step 2: Identifying Key Evaluation Questions
Step 3: Identifying Information Needed to Answer Evaluation Questions
Step 4: Evaluation Design and Methods of Data Collection

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Practical Implications

Use the theory to decide what not to measure as well as what to measure.
Tie each indicator to a decision, assumption, or causal link.
Design evaluations that can distinguish implementation issues from weak causal theory.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 378-413 Chapter 12

This note summarises Chapter 12, “Some Common Program Archetypes,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 378-413 Chapter 12

This note summarises Chapter 12, “Some Common Program Archetypes,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Core Argument
Common program archetypes help evaluators recognize recurring intervention logics, but each archetype brings different assumptions, risks, and evidence needs.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Study Summary
The chapter organizes recurring program forms such as advisory, information, education, incentives, deterrence, case management, community capacity building, and direct service delivery. These archetypes are useful because similar intervention forms often rely on similar causal assumptions.
Each archetype points to different change mechanisms. Information programs assume attention, comprehension, relevance, and ability to act. Incentives assume behavior responds to rewards. Deterrence assumes detection, sanction, and perceived risk. Case management assumes coordinated support can address multiple barriers. Capacity building assumes communities or organizations can acquire and use new capability.
The authors’ point is not to classify programs for its own sake. Archetypes help evaluators ask sharper questions about whether the chosen strategy fits the problem, what conditions are required, and what evidence would show that the mechanism is operating.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Key Concepts

Program archetype: a recurring intervention pattern with characteristic causal assumptions.
Carrots and sticks: incentive and deterrence approaches to changing behavior.
Case management: coordinated support addressing multiple participant needs or barriers.
Capacity building: strengthening ability to act at individual, organizational, or community levels.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Some Important Program Archetypes
Advisory, Information, And Education Program Archetype
Key Considerations When Using Advisory, Public Information, and Education Programs to Change Behavior

Carrot Programs to Motivate Behavior
Key Considerations When Using Carrots Program Archetype
Stick Programs to Deter Undesirable Behaviors
Key Considerations for Applying the Stick Archetype
Case Management Programs Program Archetype
Key Considerations for Applying the Archetype
Community Capacity-Building Program Archetype
Product Or Direct Service Delivery Program Archetype
Key Differences Within This Archetype for Consideration

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Practical Implications

Identify the dominant archetype before selecting indicators.
Check whether the archetype’s assumptions fit the target population and context.
Use archetypes to anticipate common failure points and unintended effects.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 320-345 Chapter 10

This note summarises Chapter 10, “Critiquing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 320-345 Chapter 10

This note summarises Chapter 10, “Critiquing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be critiqued for clarity, plausibility, evidence, context, ethics, usefulness, and proportionality before it is used as the basis for monitoring or evaluation.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The chapter provides criteria for reviewing program theory. Internal criteria examine whether the description is clear, whether the outcomes chain organizes the theory, whether outcomes address the problem, whether mechanisms are articulated, and whether the logical argument is plausible.
External validation asks whether the theory is supported by research, consistent with accepted theories, attentive to context, ethically defensible, and appropriate for its intended use. This turns program theory into an evaluand: something that can itself be assessed and improved.
The authors also emphasize stakeholder engagement and response. A critique is valuable only if its results inform revision, evaluation design, or decisions about whether the theory is good enough for the intended purpose.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Internal critique: assessment of coherence, clarity, and logical structure.
External validation: assessment against evidence, accepted theory, context, ethics, and use.
Mechanism articulation: explaining how activities or conditions produce change.
Fitness for purpose: whether the theory is worth its cost for the intended use.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Clarity of Description
The Outcomes Chain as the Central Organizing Principle for the Program Theory
Demonstration of How the Desired Outcomes Relate to Addressing the Problem
The Strength and Plausibility of the Logical Argument
Articulation of Mechanisms for Change
Criteria For External Validation
Evidence Base for the Program Theory
Consistency with Accepted Theories
Whether the Program Theory Incorporates Consideration of Its Context
The Ethical Base for the Program Theory
The Intended Use of the Program Theory
Was It Worth the Time, Effort, and Resources Given the Purposes?
Engaging Stakeholders In The Review
Responding To The Results Of A Review Of A Program Theory

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Review program theory before locking in indicators and methods.
Use critique to prioritize evidence gaps and assumptions to test.
Involve stakeholders in judging plausibility, context, and ethical acceptability.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 226-267 Chapter 8

This note summarises Chapter 8, “Developing a Theory of Action,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 226-267 Chapter 8

This note summarises Chapter 8, “Developing a Theory of Action,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Core Argument
A theory of action explains what the program will do to activate the theory of change, including resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, assumptions, and factors that affect achievement.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Study Summary
Where Chapter 7 concentrates on the desired change pathway, Chapter 8 asks how the program is expected to make that pathway happen. The theory of action identifies resources, activities, outputs, implementation features, assumptions, and external factors that influence whether intended outcomes are achieved.
The chapter gives substantial attention to success criteria and desired attributes. These convert broad outcome language into evaluative standards, making it clearer what would count as good performance. It also emphasizes realistic success criteria, especially when outcomes depend on factors outside program control.
The authors connect theory of action and theory of change through comparison: activities and outputs should be checked against the factors believed to influence outcomes. If the program does not act on the causal factors identified in the theory of change, the model exposes a strategic mismatch.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of action: the account of what the program does and how delivery is expected to support change.
Success criteria: standards or desired attributes used to judge whether outcomes are good enough.
Program factors and nonprogram factors: influences inside and outside the intervention’s control.
Theory matrix: a way to align outcomes, assumptions, activities, and evidence needs.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Chapter Structure

Preparing The Theory Of Action

Assumptions About Program Factors
Assumptions About Nonprogram External Factors
Reasons to Identify Assumptions About Factors That Will Affect Achievement of Outcomes
Managing An Identified Nonprogram Factor: Mature Workers Program
How To Identify Factors That Are Likely To Affect Outcomes
Program-Specific Research and Investigation Techniques
Program Theory and General Research
Identifying What The Program Does

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Practical Implications

Define success before interpreting evidence.
Check whether activities are logically connected to the causal factors they are meant to influence.
Track external factors that can enable, constrain, or explain outcomes.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

This note summarises Chapter 6, “Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

This note summarises Chapter 6, “Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Developing a program theory is a methodological choice, not just a workshop activity; evaluators must decide who participates, what evidence informs the theory, how conflict is handled, how much resource to invest, and when to revisit the theory.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This long chapter sets out process options for identifying or developing program theory. It contrasts staff-led, evaluator-facilitated, stakeholder-engaged, deductive, inductive, and mental-model approaches, arguing that the right mix depends on purpose, context, evidence availability, and stakeholder politics.
The authors give detailed attention to workshops and interviews, but they treat these as tools within a broader design. Workshops can surface divergent theories, build ownership, and negotiate shared models; they can also suppress disagreement if poorly facilitated. Interviews and document review may be better when perspectives are sensitive or dispersed.
The chapter is also realistic about challenges: conflicting program theories, little research evidence, reluctance to disclose assumptions, weak engagement, and limited resources. The response is not to avoid theory development but to make trade-offs explicit and revisit the theory as evidence and circumstances change.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Mental models: implicit stakeholder beliefs about how the program works.
Deductive development: building from formal theory, prior research, or established causal knowledge.
Inductive development: building from observations, cases, data, and practice experience.
Relative program theory: a theory that is fit for a purpose and moment rather than final for all uses.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Process Options For Developing Program Theory
Decision 1: Who Should Be Involved In Developing A Program Theory, And How?
Development of Program Theories by Staff, Funders, and Others
Facilitation or Development by Program Theorists and Evaluators
Decisions About Other Stakeholders to Involve
Decision 2: What Is An Appropriate Mix Of Approaches For Developing Or Eliciting The Program Theory?
Articulating Stakeholder Mental Models
Deductive Development
Inductive Development
Choosing an Approach: Articulating Mental Models, Deductive Approaches, or Inductive Approaches?
An Iterative Process Combining All Three Approaches To Program Theory Building
Implications of Choice of Approach for Who Should Be Involved
Decision 3: How Might Workshops And Interviews Be Used In Developing Program Theory?
Workshop Processes

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Design the theory-development process as carefully as the representation.
Combine stakeholder knowledge, research evidence, and empirical observation where possible.
Document disagreements and uncertainty instead of forcing premature consensus.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 82-95 Chapter 4

This note summarises Chapter 4, “Scoping Intended Uses,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 82-95 Chapter 4

This note summarises Chapter 4, “Scoping Intended Uses,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be scoped around intended users, uses, and timing; the same intervention can need different theories for planning, communication, management, monitoring, evaluation, or evidence-informed policy.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shifts attention from what a program theory contains to what it is for. A theory intended for early design and stakeholder alignment may need different detail from one used for performance monitoring, impact evaluation, or synthesis across programs.
The authors emphasize users as well as uses. Funders, managers, delivery staff, partners, participants, evaluators, and policymakers may need different representations and may also bring different knowledge about how the intervention is expected to work. Timing matters because a theory built before implementation supports design choices, while a theory reconstructed later supports evaluation and learning from what actually happened.
The chapter presents program theory as a flexible tool across the program cycle. Its usefulness depends on making the intended use explicit before deciding who participates, what evidence is needed, how detailed the model should be, and how formal the representation needs to become.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Key Concepts

Intended users: people who will directly use the program theory or evaluation findings.
Intended uses: the decisions, actions, or learning processes the theory should support.
Timing: the point in the program cycle when the theory is developed or revised.
Use-sensitive design: tailoring theory development and representation to practical use.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Why Intended Use Matters
Considering Intended Users
Considering Timing
Considering Intended and Other Possible Uses
Using Program Theory
Using Program Theory for Planning
Using Program Theory for Managing, Engaging Stakeholders, and Communicating
Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation
Using Program Theory for Evidence-Based Policy and Practice
Summary
Exercises

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Practical Implications

Begin each program theory task with a use case, not a template.
Identify who must understand, own, challenge, or apply the theory.
Revise the theory when the program moves from design to implementation to evaluation.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

This note summarises Chapter 2, “Variations of Program Theory over Time,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

This note summarises Chapter 2, “Variations of Program Theory over Time,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Core Argument
Program theory is not a new invention but a family of related ideas with varied terminology, intellectual roots, and practical traditions; evaluators need to understand the vocabulary well enough to choose terms that support use rather than confusion.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Study Summary
The chapter places program theory in a longer history of evaluation, planning, social science, and management practice. Rather than presenting one settled method, it shows how different traditions have emphasized causal explanation, planning logic, accountability, learning, stakeholder communication, and evidence synthesis.
A substantial part of the chapter is terminological. Terms such as program theory, theory of change, theory of action, logic model, logframe, results chain, outcomes chain, and evaluation model are used differently across organizations and countries. The authors treat terminology as consequential because vocabulary shapes what people attend to and what they overlook.
The chapter distinguishes key ideas without insisting on one universal label. The practical message is to define terms explicitly in each project, align language with users, and avoid assuming that a familiar diagram or phrase carries the same meaning for every stakeholder.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of change: the causal account of how and why change is expected to happen.
Theory of action: the account of what the program will do to activate or support the theory of change.
Logic model: a representation of program components and expected links, often but not always diagrammatic.
Terminological fit: choosing words that make the theory usable for the intended audience.

Australia: AusAID (Australian Agency for International Development) (Logframe)
Canada: Treasury
India: Comptroller and Auditor-General
South Africa: Framework for Managing Programme Performance Information, National Treasury
United States: University of Wisconsin Extension Service

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Practical Implications

Define the working vocabulary at the start of a program theory exercise.
Expect organizational templates to encode assumptions about what counts as a good theory.
Treat debates over terms as design signals, not merely semantic disputes.

title: Notes
text:
Evaluation notes

Notes overview
This section collects source-grounded study notes. The first collection covers Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

Book notes Theory of change Logic models Export BibTeX

  Type
    
      All types
      Chapter
      Synthesis
    
  





BookSynthesis


Purposeful Program Theory
Book overview, part structure, and reading routes for the chapter notes.




Synthesis


Core Concepts
A consolidated guide to the book’s central distinctions and recurring concepts.




Part 1Chapter


1. The Essence of Program Theory
Program theory turns evaluation from a simple before-and-after judgement into an explanation of how an intervention is expected to generate change, where that explanation might fail, and what evidence would distinguish success, partial success, implementation failure, and theory failure.




Part 1Chapter


2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
Program theory is not a new invention but a family of related ideas with varied terminology, intellectual roots, and practical traditions; evaluators need to understand the vocabulary well enough to choose terms that support use rather than confusion.




Part 1Chapter


3. Common Myths and Traps
The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose.




Part 2Chapter


4. Scoping Intended Uses
A program theory should be scoped around intended users, uses, and timing; the same intervention can need different theories for planning, communication, management, monitoring, evaluation, or evidence-informed policy.




Part 2Chapter


5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
The nature of the intervention and its setting should shape the program theory; simple, complicated, and complex situations require different assumptions about focus, governance, consistency, necessity, sufficiency, and change trajectories.




Part 3Chapter


6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
Developing a program theory is a methodological choice, not just a workshop activity; evaluators must decide who participates, what evidence informs the theory, how conflict is handled, how much resource to invest, and when to revisit the theory.




Part 3Chapter


7. Developing a Theory of Change
A theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem analysis to intended outcomes; it requires situation analysis, evidence-informed causal reasoning, careful focusing, and a defensible outcomes chain.




Part 3Chapter


8. Developing a Theory of Action
A theory of action explains what the program will do to activate the theory of change, including resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, assumptions, and factors that affect achievement.




Part 3Chapter


9. Representing Program Theory
Representing program theory is a design choice: diagrams, matrices, narratives, and multiple models should be selected to fit the intervention’s causal structure, complexity, and intended use.




Part 3Chapter


10. Critiquing Program Theory
A program theory should be critiqued for clarity, plausibility, evidence, context, ethics, usefulness, and proportionality before it is used as the basis for monitoring or evaluation.




Part 4Chapter


11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
Research-based theories of change can strengthen program theory when they are selected critically and adapted to the intervention context rather than imported mechanically.




Part 4Chapter


12. Some Common Program Archetypes
Common program archetypes help evaluators recognize recurring intervention logics, but each archetype brings different assumptions, risks, and evidence needs.




Part 4Chapter


13. Logic Models Resources
Logic model resources and technologies can support representation, but the usefulness of a logic model depends more on conceptual clarity than on the drawing tool.




Part 5Chapter


14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
Program theory can anchor monitoring and evaluation plans by identifying what to measure, when to measure it, what comparisons are needed, and how evaluation questions connect to the expected causal pathway.




Part 5Chapter


15. Causal Inference
Causal inference in program theory should be both scientific and pragmatic, combining congruence analysis, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review to assess whether the program plausibly contributed to observed outcomes.




Part 5Chapter


16. Synthesis and Reporting
Synthesis and reporting should use program theory to integrate diverse evidence, explain causal contribution, support timely learning, and connect findings across linked evaluations.









References

Funnell, Sue C., and Patricia J. Rogers. 2011. Purposeful Program Theory: Effective Use of Theories of Change and Logic Models. San Francisco, CA: Jossey-Bass.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 30-41 Chapter 1

This note summarises Chapter 1, “The Essence of Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 30-41 Chapter 1

This note summarises Chapter 1, “The Essence of Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Program theory turns evaluation from a simple before-and-after judgement into an explanation of how an intervention is expected to generate change, where that explanation might fail, and what evidence would distinguish success, partial success, implementation failure, and theory failure.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The opening chapter uses a deliberately simple health example to show why an evaluation without program theory can identify an outcome pattern but cannot say much about why it occurred. A black-box evaluation can report whether results changed, but it gives weak guidance about which mechanisms worked, which activities mattered, or what should be changed next.
The chapter then reframes evaluation around a model of how resources, activities, outputs, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, assumptions, and contextual factors are connected. This makes the evaluation more diagnostic: results can be interpreted against the expected chain of contribution rather than treated as a single program-versus-outcome comparison.
A central practical point is that failure, partial success, and apparent success all need explanation. Program theory helps evaluators identify whether the intervention was implemented as intended, whether the causal logic was plausible, whether context disrupted the pathway, and whether observed results can be credibly attributed to the program.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Black-box evaluation: an evaluation that relates an intervention to outcomes without unpacking the intervening causal pathway.
Program theory: an explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to intended outcomes.
Implementation failure versus theory failure: a poor result may reflect weak delivery, weak causal assumptions, or both.
Partial success: evidence may support some links in the chain while exposing weak or missing links elsewhere.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Evaluation Without Program Theory
Evaluation With Program Theory
Learning from Failure
Learning from Partial Success
Learning from Success
Learning from “An Apple a Day”
Summary
Exercises

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Start evaluation design by asking what must be true for the intervention to work.
Collect evidence along the causal chain, not only at the final outcome.
Use the theory to diagnose why results occurred and what should be improved.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 64-81 Chapter 3

This note summarises Chapter 3, “Common Myths and Traps,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 64-81 Chapter 3

This note summarises Chapter 3, “Common Myths and Traps,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Core Argument
The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Study Summary
The chapter clears away myths that weaken practice: program theory is not brand new, there is not one correct way to do it, it can be a serious evaluation model, and it does not necessarily require excessive time. The authors also challenge the idea that a theory is untestable or that causality can simply be assumed once a model is drawn.
The traps are more operational. Program theory can become a compliance artifact, a vague outcomes list, an overcomplicated wall chart, or a model that ignores unintended outcomes and contextual variation. A particularly important trap is developing a theory for an evaluation and then failing to use it in evaluation design, data collection, analysis, and reporting.
The chapter’s stance is pragmatic: program theory should be purposeful, proportionate, explicit, and used. A modest but well-targeted theory is better than an elaborate representation that nobody uses to make decisions.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Key Concepts

Myth: a mistaken belief about what program theory is or requires.
Trap: a recurring practice problem that undermines the usefulness of a program theory.
Purposeful proportionality: investing enough effort for the intended use without turning the exercise into an end in itself.
Unintended results: outcomes that are outside the intended chain but still matter for evaluation.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Some Common Myths
Myth 1: A New Approach
Myth 2: One Way to Do It
Myth 3: Not a Good Evaluation Model
Myth 4: Too Much Time
Myth 5: Just Draw It
Myth 6: Can’t Really Test It
Myth 7: Assume Causality
Traps To Avoid When Developing And Using Program Theory
Strategies to Avoid This Trap
Trap 2: Having a Poor Theory of Change
Trap 3: Poorly Specifying Intended Results
Trap 4: Ignoring Unintended Results
Trap 5: Oversimplifying

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Practical Implications

Choose the level of detail to match the decision or evaluation use.
Build in explicit attention to unintended outcomes and alternative explanations.
Make sure the final theory controls actual choices about measures, methods, and reporting.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention

Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 96-121 Chapter 5

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 96-121 Chapter 5

This note summarises Chapter 5, “The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Core Argument
The nature of the intervention and its setting should shape the program theory; simple, complicated, and complex situations require different assumptions about focus, governance, consistency, necessity, sufficiency, and change trajectories.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Study Summary
The chapter introduces distinctions among simple, complicated, and complex interventions. Simple situations have relatively direct and stable pathways. Complicated situations involve multiple components, actors, sites, or technical dependencies. Complex situations involve adaptation, emergence, feedback, and context-sensitive pathways.
The authors use these distinctions to warn against forcing every intervention into a linear model. A program theory may need to show multiple strands, variable implementation, different causal mechanisms in different settings, or pathways that emerge through learning. Governance and consistency also matter because centrally controlled programs differ from networked or locally adapted interventions.
The chapter’s practical value is diagnostic. Before choosing a representation or evaluation design, evaluators should ask what kind of intervention they are dealing with and what type of causal claim is reasonable.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Key Concepts

Simple, complicated, complex: a continuum for thinking about intervention structure and causal predictability.
Necessariness and sufficiency: whether a component is required for success and whether it can generate success on its own.
Change trajectory: the expected pattern and timing of change.
Governance and consistency: how control, variation, and adaptation affect the theory.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention

Summary

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Practical Implications

Do not use a single linear chain when the intervention has interacting strands or adaptive pathways.
Use context and governance analysis to decide how general the theory can be.
Treat complexity as a design issue for monitoring and evaluation, not an excuse for vagueness.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 176-225 Chapter 7

This note summarises Chapter 7, “Developing a Theory of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 176-225 Chapter 7

This note summarises Chapter 7, “Developing a Theory of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Core Argument
A theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem analysis to intended outcomes; it requires situation analysis, evidence-informed causal reasoning, careful focusing, and a defensible outcomes chain.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Study Summary
The chapter focuses on the change side of program theory: what problem is being addressed, what causes or contributing factors sustain it, what opportunities can be used, and what sequence of outcomes would indicate meaningful change. Situation analysis is presented as the foundation for a credible theory of change.
The authors stress that causes, consequences, and opportunities should be investigated rather than assumed. Research evidence can inform which causal factors are plausible and which strategies are likely to influence them. Focusing and scoping are then needed because no program can address every cause, consequence, population, and context at once.
The outcomes chain is the central device in the chapter. It connects early, intermediate, and long-term outcomes in a way that shows consequential progression rather than just listing desired results. A good outcomes chain makes the causal story inspectable and testable.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Key Concepts

Situation analysis: structured understanding of the problem, causes, consequences, and opportunities.
Outcomes chain: a sequence of linked outcomes showing expected progression toward longer-term change.
Focusing and scoping: deliberate choices about what the program will and will not address.
Causal plausibility: the extent to which the proposed pathway is supported by reasoning and evidence.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Features Of A Theory Of Change
Situation Analysis: Understanding The Problem, Its Causes, And Its Consequences
What a Situation Analysis Is
Causes, Contributing Factors, And Opportunities
Why a Situation Analysis Should Be Done
When and by Whom a Situation Analysis Should Be Done
Questions for Preparing a Situation Analysis
The Nature and Extent of the Main Problem That the Program Addresses
Causes and Contributing Factors
Consequences
How the Use of These Questions Might Vary with Choice of Program Theory Development Process
Using Research To Identify Causes And Select Strategies
Assessing Complication and Complexity
Focusing And Scoping

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Practical Implications

Do not start with activities; start with the problem and causal conditions.
Use research and local evidence to identify leverage points.
Build outcomes chains that distinguish immediate outputs from meaningful changes in people, systems, or conditions.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 268-319 Chapter 9

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 268-319 Chapter 9

This note summarises Chapter 9, “Representing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Representing program theory is a design choice: diagrams, matrices, narratives, and multiple models should be selected to fit the intervention’s causal structure, complexity, and intended use.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This chapter surveys representation options, including pipeline logic models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and multiple linked models. It treats representation as more than presentation: the form chosen shapes what users notice, question, and can use.
The authors distinguish representations for complicated and complex programs. Multiple organizations, sequential interventions, parallel causal strands, context-specific mechanisms, emergent theory of action, vertical integration, and systems concepts may all require alternatives to a single diagram.
The chapter ends with practical quality criteria. Good representations explain contribution, avoid dead ends, make arrows meaningful, show direction of change, focus on key elements, avoid clutter, and remove material that does not add meaning. The test is whether the representation clarifies the theory for its intended use.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Pipeline logic model: a representation moving from inputs and activities through outputs and outcomes.
Outcomes chain model: a representation centered on consequential outcome progression.
Realist matrix: a representation that links context, mechanism, and outcome patterns.
Representational economy: including enough to guide use without overwhelming interpretation.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Options For Representation
A Pipeline Logic Model of a Computer Project
An Outcomes Chain Logic Model of a Computer Project
Comparing Pipeline Models and Outcomes Chain Models
Realist Matrices
Narratives
Developing More Than One Logic Model
Options for Formatting Logic Models
Representing Complicated Program Theory
Multiple Organizations Working in Parallel
Multiple Interventions Working Together Sequentially
Multiple Causal Strands Within an Intervention
Interventions That Work Only in Particular Contexts
Representing Complex Program Theory

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Choose representation after clarifying use and intervention complexity.
Use multiple models when one view cannot serve all users or levels.
Review diagrams for meaningful arrows, missing assumptions, and avoidable clutter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 346-377 Chapter 11

This note summarises Chapter 11, “Some Research-Based Theories of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 346-377 Chapter 11

This note summarises Chapter 11, “Some Research-Based Theories of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Core Argument
Research-based theories of change can strengthen program theory when they are selected critically and adapted to the intervention context rather than imported mechanically.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Study Summary
This chapter introduces several families of research-based theory, including reasoned action and planned behavior, stages of change, empowerment, diffusion, socioecological theory, and network theory. Each offers concepts that can help explain how change might occur.
The authors do not present these theories as universal templates. Each has limits, assumptions, and domains of usefulness. The task is to use research-based theory to sharpen causal reasoning, identify intermediate outcomes, and choose measures, while still checking whether the theory fits the specific program and context.
The chapter is especially useful as a bridge between general social science and practical evaluation. It shows how evaluators can use established theory to avoid purely local speculation without losing attention to implementation realities.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Key Concepts

Research-based theory: formal or empirical theory that can inform causal assumptions in a program theory.
Diffusion: the spread of ideas or practices through populations, networks, or systems.
Socioecological theory: attention to multiple levels of influence, from individual to institutional and environmental.
Theory selection: choosing concepts because they explain the intervention, not because they are familiar.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Theory Of Reasoned Action And Theory Of Planned Behavior
Some Theory Limitations
Relevance to Building a Program Theory and Measuring Outcomes
Stages Of Change Theory
Some Limitations
Empowerment Theory
Diffusion Theory
Key Concepts and Related Theories
Relevance for Building a Program Theory and Measuring Outcomes
Socioecological Theory
Network Theory
Selecting And Using Theories Of Change
Summary
Exercises

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Practical Implications

Use formal theory to identify mechanisms, assumptions, and intermediate outcomes.
State the limitations of imported theory and adapt it to context.
Avoid mixing theories without explaining how their mechanisms relate.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 414-443 Chapter 13

This note summarises Chapter 13, “Logic Models Resources,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Core Argument
Logic model resources and technologies can support representation, but the usefulness of a logic model depends more on conceptual clarity than on the drawing tool.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Study Summary
This chapter reviews pipeline logic models, variations of outcomes chain models, drawn models, standard software, specialist visual tools, and dedicated logic model packages. It gives evaluators a practical sense of available representation formats.
The chapter reinforces earlier cautions: a visually tidy model can still be conceptually weak, and a complex tool can obscure rather than clarify the theory. Technology should support the intended use, audience, and complexity of the program theory.
The practical emphasis is on choosing the right representation environment. Some situations need quick sketches to support dialogue; others need formal models for documentation, monitoring, or reporting. The tool should make revision, communication, and use easier.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Key Concepts

Pipeline model variation: different ways to organize inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes.
Outcomes-chain variation: different degrees of detail, nesting, and temporal structure in outcome pathways.
Representation technology: software or visual methods used to create and maintain logic models.
Tool fit: alignment between representation medium, audience, and purpose.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Pipeline Logic Models
Ways in Which Pipeline Logic Models Vary
Variations of Pipeline Logic Models
Variations Of Outcomes Chain Logic Models
Technology For Representing Program Theory
Drawn Logic Models
Standard Computer Packages
Long-Term
Outcomes
Outcomes
Short-
Term
Outputs
Processes

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Practical Implications

Select tools after clarifying users and revision needs.
Do not let software templates dictate the causal logic.
Maintain models in formats that can be updated as evidence and use evolve.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 496-527 Chapter 15

This note summarises Chapter 15, “Causal Inference,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 496-527 Chapter 15

This note summarises Chapter 15, “Causal Inference,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Core Argument
Causal inference in program theory should be both scientific and pragmatic, combining congruence analysis, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review to assess whether the program plausibly contributed to observed outcomes.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Study Summary
The chapter argues against polarized thinking about causal inference. Strong causal analysis requires rigor, but rigor does not always mean one design. Program theory helps evaluators choose and combine methods that are appropriate to the intervention, question, and available evidence.
The authors organize causal analysis around congruence, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review. Congruence examines whether observed patterns match the theory across intermediate and final outcomes. Counterfactual comparisons ask what would have happened without the program. Critical review tests alternative explanations, exceptions, and weak links.
The chapter is especially important for complicated and complex interventions, where single average effects may hide variation. Program theory supports disaggregation, mechanism testing, and careful explanation of why outcomes occurred for whom and under what conditions.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Key Concepts

Congruence analysis: checking whether evidence across the causal chain fits the program theory.
Counterfactual comparison: estimating what would have happened in the absence of the intervention.
Critical review: actively examining alternative explanations and exceptions.
Scientific pragmatism: using credible methods that fit the real evaluation problem.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

The Need To Be Scientific And Pragmatic
Myths About Causal Inference
Clarifying Terminology
Being Scientific
Being Pragmatic
A Framework For Causal Analysis Using Program Theory
Congruence
Comparing Achievement of Intermediate and Final Outcomes
Methods for Investigating Implementation and Theory Failure
Investigating Implementation Failure Of A Nutritional Program In Bangladesh
Disaggregating Results for Complicated Interventions
Comparing the Statistical Model and Actual Results
Asking Participants
Counterfactual Comparisons

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Practical Implications

Do not treat program theory as a substitute for causal evidence; use it to structure causal tests.
Combine pattern evidence, comparison evidence, and alternative-explanation review.
Disaggregate results where the theory predicts different pathways or effects.

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This synthesis draws on the full set of chapter notes for Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This synthesis draws on the full set of chapter notes for Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Practical Habits

Start by naming the intended users, uses, and timing of the program theory.
Separate the problem analysis and desired change pathway from the program’s activities and delivery model.
Make assumptions explicit enough that they can be tested or monitored.
Choose representation formats for use, not for visual polish.
Critique the theory before using it to select indicators or evaluation methods.
Use the theory to distinguish implementation failure, theory failure, contextual disruption, and partial success.
Report evidence along the causal pathway so conclusions are traceable.

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Design Checklist

What decision or learning need should the program theory support?
Who needs to participate in developing, challenging, or using it?
What problem, causes, contributing factors, and opportunities does the theory identify?
Which outcomes form a genuine causal chain rather than a list of aspirations?
Which activities and outputs are expected to influence the causal factors?
What contextual or external factors could enable or block outcomes?
Which links in the theory are most uncertain, consequential, or worth testing?
What comparison, congruence evidence, or critical review is needed for causal inference?
